T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶ 28/09/2025 8:55
Head to Board Game Geek for the latest rulebook and codex: Rulebook: https://boardgamegeek.com/filepage/276609/compile-english-rulebook Codex: https://boardgamegeek.com/filepage/291919/compile-codex Note: you may need a BGG account and may have to be logged in to access the files. (edited)
Michael Yang 04/10/2025 18:20
I meant to post a small rule change here that I put into the last Codex update. The player compiling deletes all cards from the line. Originally, the rules for compiling were written such that the compiling player deleted all of their cards and then the opposing player deleted theirs. Helpful for teaching the game, but ultimately unhealthy for design space.
1

StGrimblefig 15/10/2025 22:15
Regarding the Aux2 preview, if Unity 1 center command is activated after Unity is compiled, does anything happen? Do you still delete the cards? I presume that the 'recompile' rule is not applicable, since it does not say "compile the Unity line."
klenz 15/10/2025 22:20
Well, the flipping of the Unity protocol to "compiled" side can not be done, but everything else should work just like a regular re-compile?
Just Plain Mat 15/10/2025 22:24
The Unity card doesn't actually say to "Compile the line" only to flip the Unity Protocol to the Compiled side. This means to me that you are not following the normal Compile/Recompile action so in the case of recompile you would not be able to pick up a card from your opponent's deck. However the card does still state to Delete all cards in the line so that would still be done regardless if the Protocol was flipped or not
klenz 15/10/2025 22:26
Oh thats right.
Just Plain Mat
The Unity card doesn't actually say to "Compile the line" only to flip the Unity Protocol to the Compiled side. This means to me that you are not following the normal Compile/Recompile action so in the case of recompile you would not be able to pick up a card from your opponent's deck. However the card does still state to Delete all cards in the line so that would still be done regardless if the Protocol was flipped or not
StGrimblefig 15/10/2025 22:27
That's how I read it, because of it being a single command with 'and' in the middle.
klenz 15/10/2025 22:27
Looks like there will be more "clarifications" added to the compile codex for UNITY 1 for sure
Michael Yang 15/10/2025 23:27
Ya'll got it right. Unity 1 can't recompile but always deletes when triggered.

Sycopath 16/10/2025 21:38
Ok, order of operations question: I’m holding 1 card in hand. hate 3 is on my board and plague 1 is on my opponent’s covered by plague 2 and another card. Hate 0 is being covered by a facedown card and I flip hate 2 face up in a different row from hate 0. Hate 2 is tied with the face down covering hate 0 for highest on my board. My opponent will only ever discard 1 card to plague 2. assuming that the order of resolution ends up being: hate 2 self delete, hate 0 opponent delete, plague 2 discard cards, hate 2 opponent delete, plague 1 discard. How many cards will I draw, how many will my opponent draw, and when for each. Tl;dr how “after” is after and which after triggers can I order as I see fit. Will happily provide a picture of the setup later
Michael Yang 16/10/2025 21:41
<hate 3> <plague 1> <plague 2> <hate 0> <hate 2>
21:47
It's a little hard to read through your situation. Maybe I can address the core issue. "After" means the thing and all of its consequences are done. So, Hate 2 deletes the card on top of Hate 0 which then triggers Hate 0's delete. You then finish Hate 0's delete and draw a card for this one from Hate 3. Then, after this, Hate 3 would draw a card from Hate 2's delete. Because the 2nd delete is a consequence of the first, it completes before the 1st delete does.
21:50
Let me know if this doesn't help and I'll try again.
Sycopath
Ok, order of operations question: I’m holding 1 card in hand. hate 3 is on my board and plague 1 is on my opponent’s covered by plague 2 and another card. Hate 0 is being covered by a facedown card and I flip hate 2 face up in a different row from hate 0. Hate 2 is tied with the face down covering hate 0 for highest on my board. My opponent will only ever discard 1 card to plague 2. assuming that the order of resolution ends up being: hate 2 self delete, hate 0 opponent delete, plague 2 discard cards, hate 2 opponent delete, plague 1 discard. How many cards will I draw, how many will my opponent draw, and when for each. Tl;dr how “after” is after and which after triggers can I order as I see fit. Will happily provide a picture of the setup later

Michael Yang 16/10/2025 22:33
I really love this idea. I use this if anyone wants to make something of it: (0: H2, 1: H3, H0, fd) (3: Pl1, 5: Pl4) I usually write notes to myself with something like this. Each stack is numbered: 0 is Player 1's left stack, 2 is their right stack, 3 is the stack opposite 0, and 5 is the stack opposite 2. If the stack ID is in the list, then we know it has cards in it. Cards are ordered in a stack from first to last; the last card being the uncovered card in the stack. In this example, H = Hate, Pl = Plague, and fd = face-down card.
2
1

Eris 16/10/2025 22:40
A quick question to make sure: if my "End:" effect uncovers a card with an "End:" effect, does this second command trigger?
Eris
A quick question to make sure: if my "End:" effect uncovers a card with an "End:" effect, does this second command trigger?
T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶ 16/10/2025 22:41
It does not. The End effect has to be visible at the start of the End step to go off.
Eris 16/10/2025 22:41
Perfect thanks
Sycopath
Ok, order of operations question: I’m holding 1 card in hand. hate 3 is on my board and plague 1 is on my opponent’s covered by plague 2 and another card. Hate 0 is being covered by a facedown card and I flip hate 2 face up in a different row from hate 0. Hate 2 is tied with the face down covering hate 0 for highest on my board. My opponent will only ever discard 1 card to plague 2. assuming that the order of resolution ends up being: hate 2 self delete, hate 0 opponent delete, plague 2 discard cards, hate 2 opponent delete, plague 1 discard. How many cards will I draw, how many will my opponent draw, and when for each. Tl;dr how “after” is after and which after triggers can I order as I see fit. Will happily provide a picture of the setup later
Michael Yang 16/10/2025 22:46
I think this covers the whole series. Definitely one of the more complicated situations in the game. I shudder at the thought of a Spirit 3 being in play during this. I play Hate 2 Hate 2 middle command triggers Hate 2 deletes face-down card Hate 0 is uncovered Hate 0 deletes Plague 5 Plague 2 middle command triggers I discard cards Plague 1 top command triggers Opponent draws 1 card Hate 0 delete is done Hate 3 top command triggers I draw 1 card Hate 2 delete is done Hate 3 top command triggers I draw 1 card Hate 2 does its second delete Plague 2 is deleted Plague 1 is uncovered Plague 1 middle command triggers I discard a card Plague 1 top command triggers Opponent draws 1 card Hate 2 second delete is done Hate 3 top command triggers I draw 1 card
4
2
2
1
klenz 16/10/2025 22:46
We could also use all that errata and clarifications as a simple website, where we can link to exact cases. PDF is good to read hard to use as a quick reference though. (edited)
Eris
A quick question to make sure: if my "End:" effect uncovers a card with an "End:" effect, does this second command trigger?
Michael Yang 16/10/2025 22:47
Right. All of your End triggers happen right after you enter the End step and that's it
Sycopath 16/10/2025 22:47
Thank you!!!
klenz 16/10/2025 22:48
And that sequence is a great example of why i love this game so much
Michael Yang
I think this covers the whole series. Definitely one of the more complicated situations in the game. I shudder at the thought of a Spirit 3 being in play during this. I play Hate 2 Hate 2 middle command triggers Hate 2 deletes face-down card Hate 0 is uncovered Hate 0 deletes Plague 5 Plague 2 middle command triggers I discard cards Plague 1 top command triggers Opponent draws 1 card Hate 0 delete is done Hate 3 top command triggers I draw 1 card Hate 2 delete is done Hate 3 top command triggers I draw 1 card Hate 2 does its second delete Plague 2 is deleted Plague 1 is uncovered Plague 1 middle command triggers I discard a card Plague 1 top command triggers Opponent draws 1 card Hate 2 second delete is done Hate 3 top command triggers I draw 1 card
Eris 16/10/2025 22:51
I love hate
Michael Yang 16/10/2025 22:52
Working on this kind of puzzle by myself is fun but it pales in comparison to puzzling through something like this with your partner during a game. It's so fun to come across stuff like this that just feels kind of magical and then you both explore it together
Michael Yang
Right. All of your End triggers happen right after you enter the End step and that's it
PW 16/10/2025 23:52
@Michael Yang but the second End is uncovered after the end phase has begun so why does it trigger, i thought that you note all End triggers at the beginning of the End phase and the ones "arriving" during the End phase do not trigger, there's something i don't understand in your answer (but i am probably wrong) (edited)
Michael Yang 16/10/2025 23:55
I think I quoted the wrong message. You're understanding is correct. You enter the End phase, all End: stuff triggers, then you choose the order that they resolve. Any End: triggers that come up after that first part, doesn't matter (edited)
1
PW 16/10/2025 23:59
ok thanks for your answer and just to be sure, it means that if a card with an End effect is covered during the end phase by another end phase effect then, even covered, you trigger it's effect (as you noted it at the beginning of the end phase) ? is that right @Michael Yang ? (edited)
Michael Yang 17/10/2025 0:02
No. Covering the text cancels it. If it gets uncovered again, nothing happens because it's text was initially cancelled and we're past the window where the End: commands would trigger.
1
PW 17/10/2025 0:03
@Michael Yang ok thanks, i asked because i thought that this could happen but i was not sure because the codex says that you note all the end triggers... (edited)

Just Plain Mat 17/10/2025 4:14

I really love this idea. I use this if anyone wants to make something of it: (0: H2, 1: H3, H0, fd) (3: Pl1, 5: Pl4) I usually write notes to myself with something like this. Each stack is numbered: 0 is Player 1's left stack, 2 is their right stack, 3 is the stack opposite 0, and 5 is the stack opposite 2. If the stack ID is in the list, then we know it has cards in it. Cards are ordered in a stack from first to last; the last card being the uncovered card in the stack. In this example, H = Hate, Pl = Plague, and fd = face-down card.
Wallstormer 17/10/2025 20:37
Oooh, chess-style notation comes to Compile...I love it, lol. I wonder about having notation for Stacks be a bit more contextual? 0a and 0b for Line 1 instead of 0 and 1 would make it clearer at a glance that those stacks are in opposition with each other, and also make adjacent lines clearer too (Stack 1a/b are adjacent to Line 0 and Line 2, instead of the extra mental step of remembering that Stack 1 and Stack 4 are the middle line) (edited)
1
Michael Yang 17/10/2025 20:45
That's definitely better for humans. The numbers are nice for indexing in a list but that's really it

Hello, I'm excited for getting my compile. And I have a small rule question. Is rearrange action instant? In other words, there is no middle state that two or more cards is uncovered.

machmo99 26/10/2025 21:42
Hey idk if this question has been already answered, but if you shift an covered card, whats the new position? Do you place it at the bottom of the stack (now uncovered) or at the relative position of the original spot? (edited)
T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶ 26/10/2025 21:44
I assume by "deck" you mean stack- when you shift any card to a new line/stack, it becomes the new uncovered card of that stack
T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶
I assume by "deck" you mean stack- when you shift any card to a new line/stack, it becomes the new uncovered card of that stack
machmo99 26/10/2025 21:44

.Languste. 29/10/2025 21:39
We just had this situation where the Luck 3 effect i played isnt so benefitial for me. Hence this question came up: Regarding "State a protocol", am i allowed to state a protocol that is not currently in our game (so I gurantee my guess is wrong)?
T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶ 29/10/2025 21:40
Ooo, interesting. Never considered that, lol
Funny Samy 29/10/2025 21:40
I think similar things have resolved to no but this seems different lol
21:41
You can definitely name one of your own protocols assuming the opponent hasn't recompiled a bunch or something
Just Plain Mat 29/10/2025 21:41
I definitely don't think you can state a Protocol that's not currently in game. However I think the next best thing would be to state on of your protocols since that's always a possiblity
T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶ 29/10/2025 21:42
It doesnt even say state an opponent Protocol, and there are some situations where you might not know what an opponent could have if they stole your card and shuffled it into the deck... So you I imagine stating one of your own protocols would be legal
Funny Samy 29/10/2025 21:42
You can definitely state your own protocols because it's very possible that would hit
Just Plain Mat 29/10/2025 21:43
Although to nitpick I feel like stating your own protocol when you know it's not in your opponents deck would go against the idea of the "Luck" protocol.
T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶ 29/10/2025 21:46
I dont disagree, but I dont know that that should factor into whether you can or not- if that wasnt the intent then the card should have specified "opponent protocols", or we need a new ruling, lol. But a ruling for something like this could get a little weird
Funny Samy 29/10/2025 21:47
I don't think it should limit to opponent protocols
21:48
Imagine the hype of correctly hitting with one of your own (also would suck if it is one of yours and there's just no possibility of hitting)
2
21:48
If it were up to me the ruling would just be you have to state one of the six protocols in the current game
3

.Languste.
We just had this situation where the Luck 3 effect i played isnt so benefitial for me. Hence this question came up: Regarding "State a protocol", am i allowed to state a protocol that is not currently in our game (so I gurantee my guess is wrong)?
Michael Yang 29/10/2025 22:18
Yes, you can state any protocol, even one not in the game. I don't know if it'll stay like that but I'm happy with the ruling now. I don't see any compelling reason to limit it since it's a presumably beneficial effect. I don't have any other cards in the pipeline that use a similar mechanic but I reserve the right to change my mind if I revisit the idea.

Michael Yang
So, I realize that this is fine in the physical version but terrible in a digital implementation. Will likely have to state a protocol in the game. Right now, the implementation is unambiguous - any protocol can be stated - so I'll have to figure the way to handle it.
BlueMoons 29/10/2025 23:10

23:12
unless you specifically want a popup, people know how to use autocompletes
23:13

23:13

T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶ 29/10/2025 23:16
Hm, not a big fan of that ruling, ngl. I think theres a number of problems if you decide to go this route:
  • First off, where are the limits of what you can and cannot state? Maybe for this card, its not a big deal, but can I name Main 2 cards if I dont own them or if they havent been "released" yet? What about custom protocols? Playtested protocols?
  • Second off, youve effectively made this effect a "may" effect but in a less clean and consistent manner. The effect effectively becomes optional but without using the actual word "may", which feels strange
  • As Plain Mat stated, stating a Protocol in game but that you know your opponent doesnt have feels strange and anti-thetical to "Luck", and also to how most effects have to resolve of "having to change the state if they can". I get that this is a slightly different kind of effect, but allowing stating any protocol feels like it goes too far in the other direction
  • Wording. If you can state any protocol, even one outside of the game, when resolving "State a protocol", then why wouldnt I be able select protocols outside of the game when I "swap protocols" ? If the word "Protocol" means any protocol, even one outside of the game, then this could be a natural train of logic you can follow. Obviously thats not out we want effect like that to work, but then the word "Protocol" means two different things for two different cards, which feels weird
 (edited)
1
1
Michael Yang 29/10/2025 23:16

Michael Yang 29/10/2025 23:34
where are the limits of what you can and cannot state? The text as written feels unambiguous to me. You can state any protocol that exists in the game. youve effectively made this effect a "may" effect but in a less clean and consistent manner. That actually feels like a cool way to do a "may". stating a Protocol in game but that you know your opponent doesnt have feels strange and anti-thetical to "Luck" Yeah, definitely agree. But this was a rules questions, not a question of intention. It was my intention that you call a protocol in play but that's not what the card says. If you can state any protocol, even one outside of the game, when resolving "State a protocol", then why wouldnt I be able select protocols outside of the game when I "swap protocols" ? This is a good point. But where <spirit 4> interacts with game objects, Luck 3 uses words. If it said "Choose 1 protocol." this would be open and shut. But because you don't "state" a "protocol card" ever, we're in unprecedented territory. I don't automatically prescribe the same logic to it as I would when interacting with game objects.
Michael Yang
where are the limits of what you can and cannot state? The text as written feels unambiguous to me. You can state any protocol that exists in the game. youve effectively made this effect a "may" effect but in a less clean and consistent manner. That actually feels like a cool way to do a "may". stating a Protocol in game but that you know your opponent doesnt have feels strange and anti-thetical to "Luck" Yeah, definitely agree. But this was a rules questions, not a question of intention. It was my intention that you call a protocol in play but that's not what the card says. If you can state any protocol, even one outside of the game, when resolving "State a protocol", then why wouldnt I be able select protocols outside of the game when I "swap protocols" ? This is a good point. But where <spirit 4> interacts with game objects, Luck 3 uses words. If it said "Choose 1 protocol." this would be open and shut. But because you don't "state" a "protocol card" ever, we're in unprecedented territory. I don't automatically prescribe the same logic to it as I would when interacting with game objects.
CompileBot BOT 29/10/2025 23:34
CompileBot v1.0.2
Michael Yang 29/10/2025 23:43
This idea of "state" a thing is the problem I think. "State" isn't a verb described by the game. Originally, I had "choose" but there was disagreement at GTG about it so it was changed to "state". Here's how I'd write it: "Choose a protocol. Discard the top card of your opponent's deck. If the discarded card's protocol matched the chosen protocol, delete 1 card."

23:46

Swordodo 29/10/2025 23:46

Just Plain Mat 30/10/2025 0:00
"Choose" is a word that is used across multiple other cards, not a keyword like delete and flip but still something shared between cards. "State" is only used in two cards across all of the protocols. To me that makes "Choose" a pseudo-keywork that does keep it within the bounds and rules of the other cards
Michael Yang 30/10/2025 0:01
Mind you, I'm bringing in baggage into this conversation that none of you have context on. There was a not-so-short conversation about choose, state, say, select, name, etc. Was it necessary? Probably not. Am I forever affected by it? Definitely. (edited)

I've only had this game for a day so this is probably a dumb question but I read the Codex and I'm still not 100% sure -- If I have a card like <life 2>, and there's a face-down card covered by another card, can I still flip that face-down card face-up? I guess my confusion is that I know that card text supersedes the normal "can only target uncovered cards" restriction, but I'm not sure whether the "face down card" target supersedes the "uncovered" restriction, since it's not explicitly targeting a "covered card". (edited)
dexo568
I've only had this game for a day so this is probably a dumb question but I read the Codex and I'm still not 100% sure -- If I have a card like <life 2>, and there's a face-down card covered by another card, can I still flip that face-down card face-up? I guess my confusion is that I know that card text supersedes the normal "can only target uncovered cards" restriction, but I'm not sure whether the "face down card" target supersedes the "uncovered" restriction, since it's not explicitly targeting a "covered card". (edited)
CompileBot BOT 10/11/2025 21:26
CompileBot v1.0.2
T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶ 10/11/2025 21:27
Negative. The card you select with Life 2 has to be uncovered.
dexo568 10/11/2025 21:28
Gotcha, thanks.
T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶ 10/11/2025 21:30
The words to look out for "superseding" are "all", "this", and "that". So if an effect says "all cards", "this card" or "that card", those bypass selection.
dexo568 10/11/2025 21:31
That was my assumption, but I was getting a little tripped up because I was comparing its effect to to that of <death 2>, which I assume does delete covered cards of value 1 or 2, but I couldn't rationalize to myself why death 2 could legally target covered "1 or 2 cards" but life 2 can't legally target covered "flip cards". (edited)
dexo568
That was my assumption, but I was getting a little tripped up because I was comparing its effect to to that of <death 2>, which I assume does delete covered cards of value 1 or 2, but I couldn't rationalize to myself why death 2 could legally target covered "1 or 2 cards" but life 2 can't legally target covered "flip cards". (edited)
CompileBot BOT 10/11/2025 21:31
CompileBot v1.0.2
dexo568 10/11/2025 21:31
But if it's the "all" keyword there that's doing the superseding, that makes sense.

Very broad question but how does time 2 work
0:01
Shuffle is triggered by a draw action, but can time 2 interrupt the draw by covering it? What happens then
Bart_KF 12/11/2025 0:03
Can you post the text of the card? I don't think the new stuff is in CompileBot yet
Just Plain Mat 12/11/2025 0:04
Top: After you shuffle your deck: Draw 1 card. Then, you may shift this card. After you shuffle your deck: Draw 1 card and you may shift this card. Middle: If there are any cards in your trash, you may shuffle your trash into your deck. (edited)
0:05
Since the Draw effect is on the Top command, it will never be covered. The only way it's effect can be interrupted or turned off is if the card is flipped face-down
Funny Samy
Shuffle is triggered by a draw action, but can time 2 interrupt the draw by covering it? What happens then
BlueMoons 12/11/2025 0:06
i'm pretty sure the draw operation needing you to shuffle beforehand is atomic
0:06
the shuffle is part of your draw
0:06
just like how drawing multiple cards like in a refresh is 1 operation
0:07
unsure if that's actually how it works but i don't see how it would otherwise
0:08
also, that's not the errata'd text
0:08
the errata'd text says draw 1 card, then you may shift
0:08
it makes it clear that that's two things happening in sequence
BlueMoons
just like how drawing multiple cards like in a refresh is 1 operation
Just Plain Mat 12/11/2025 0:09
Yes, that's how it should work. Multiple cards being drawn by the same command counts as a single draw for the triggers. Like with Life 1 having "Flip 1 card. Flip 1 card." as the command so it would count as 2 distinct flips
BlueMoons 12/11/2025 0:09
you couldn't do the trigger for time 2 in between drawing 3 cards if you only have 1 in your deck (edited)
BlueMoons
also, that's not the errata'd text
Just Plain Mat 12/11/2025 0:10

BlueMoons 12/11/2025 0:10
so i can only assume you do the trigger after you're done drawing the cards (edited)
Just Plain Mat 12/11/2025 0:12
Hmmm... that's a good point. I would definitely agree that the "After you shuffle your deck" trigger would only take place after the final card is drawn post-shuffle but the phrasing on the card would suggest otherwise.
BlueMoons 12/11/2025 0:16
i imagine it won't matter how you resolve it in the vast majority of cases
Just Plain Mat 12/11/2025 0:17

Action 13/11/2025 5:54
<Mirror 2> How does the order of operations work here? If I choose to swap the stack that I'm playing Mirror 2 into, will Mirror 2 follow the stack as it's swapped? Alternatively, will the stacks swap before Mirror 2 is placed, and thereby remain in the line I decided to play it initially? (edited)
Action
<Mirror 2> How does the order of operations work here? If I choose to swap the stack that I'm playing Mirror 2 into, will Mirror 2 follow the stack as it's swapped? Alternatively, will the stacks swap before Mirror 2 is placed, and thereby remain in the line I decided to play it initially? (edited)
CompileBot BOT 13/11/2025 5:54
CompileBot v1.0.2
makario 13/11/2025 5:55
The card will move. It is impossible for this card to have moved stacks without it first being placed. The card enters the field (it "lands"), then triggers.
1
Action 13/11/2025 5:55
Excellent thank you
1
5:57
As a corollary then (having read the FAQ), if I place Mirror 2 in an empty stack, by the time the card triggers it will have made its own stack a valid target, if I understand correctly
makario 13/11/2025 5:57
You're correct
Funny Samy 13/11/2025 5:57
yeah it alone can be one of the two stacks
Action 13/11/2025 5:57
Fantastic, thank you both
makario 13/11/2025 5:57

Michael Yang
In short, nothing interrupts drawing (like Just Plain Mat said). I'll have more detailed examples by next week. Just got back home today
Colton 16/11/2025 16:03

Draw after. You'll have 6.

BlueMoons

BlueMoons 16/11/2025 20:10
let me write down the thing we were discussing here again If you've got the setup Time 0 | Ice 2 | [empty] Time 5 and then play ice 1 in the middle >> shift left >> trigger ice 2 >> shift time 5 to the middle >> trigger time 0 >> play a card in the time line because of how the committed cards stack up, you would be forced to finish the time 0 textbox and shuffle away your decck before the committed cards start landing, unlike if you played time 0 to fish up time 5 without committed cards, where it would be covered before the shuffle text goes, correct? (edited)
Funny Samy
If you could illustrate this that would be great
Michael Yang 16/11/2025 20:12
Here's the short of what I have but it's not been reviewed by the team - it'll be in the next Codex update (release TBD): Time 2’s shuffling doesn’t interrupt a draw action that would trigger it. If a draw begins and the deck is empty, the deck is shuffled, cards are drawn, and then Time 2’s “After you shuffle” command triggers. If there are some cards in the deck but not enough to complete the draw, draw as many cards as possible, shuffle the deck, complete the draw, and then trigger Time 2’s top command. In both cases, a command that happens “After you draw cards” would trigger at the same time as “After you shuffle”.
Funny Samy 16/11/2025 20:13
Interesting

Does this card want to just increase the value of its owners stack? The card text would be more intuitive to me if the word line was replaced with stack, since a line is shared by players while a stack is owned by one player. (edited)
Bart_KF 22/11/2025 6:50

BlueMoons 22/11/2025 15:58

1
StGrimblefig 23/11/2025 17:05
The things said above are correct for the current cards/rules. The wording as written leaves an opening for possible future cards/rules that allow a card or cards on your opponent's side of the field to be included in your total value. If the wording is changed to 'stack' rather than 'line,' that opening is gone.

so if the situation is you have gravity 1 covered in one line and you play gravity 2 in a different line. you shift the card covering gravity 1 off of it to the gravity 2 line.

during the shift the card is committed
4:53
at which point gravity 1 triggers
4:53
because its been uncovered
4:54

I often use "steal" when teaching recompiling because it gets at the ownership change but it does miss the draw part (edited)
Guzel 01/12/2025 11:43
If I have gravity 1 in a stack and another card covers it, then I play gravity 2 in a different stack, can I infinitely shift the card (covering gravity 1) between gravity 1 and 2 while drawing all cards in my deck? (and flipping the moving card)
11:45
Similar situation: If I have spirit 1 in play with spirit 3 covering it, if I play spirit 0 on another stack, can I shift spirit 3 between them to draw all the cards in my deck?
Guzel
If I have gravity 1 in a stack and another card covers it, then I play gravity 2 in a different stack, can I infinitely shift the card (covering gravity 1) between gravity 1 and 2 while drawing all cards in my deck? (and flipping the moving card)
makario 01/12/2025 12:36
You cannot. See the pinned section for the codex, but to explain the situation you're talking about: When gravity 2 shifts the card off of gravity 1, that card is "committed" to g2's stack but does not land yet. G1 triggers since it is now revealed, but since the card covering it is not on the field yet (it's committed... think floating above), it is not a valid target for g1. After G1 finishes, the committed card will land.
12:37
Similar situation for your spirit question
Guzel 01/12/2025 12:38
ahh so cards shifting are "in limbo" and not in play, thanks!
makario 01/12/2025 12:38
Yep!
Khrono5 01/12/2025 17:36
also recommend searching this chat, I answered this exact question last week
foxyplasma 01/12/2025 21:50

foxyplasma

CompileBot BOT 01/12/2025 21:50
CompileBot v1.0.2
makario 01/12/2025 21:51
Nope
21:53

21:55
Regardless, if the act of compiling includes both delete actions, then "after compilation" would mean that War 2 would be in the trash by the time its effect would have triggered
foxyplasma
If <war 2> is in the line my opponent is compiling, does its bottom effect happen?
Michael Yang 01/12/2025 22:16
It wouldn't trigger. The "after compile" window is after control is used, all the deletes finish, and the protocol is flipped.

makario
You cannot. See the pinned section for the codex, but to explain the situation you're talking about: When gravity 2 shifts the card off of gravity 1, that card is "committed" to g2's stack but does not land yet. G1 triggers since it is now revealed, but since the card covering it is not on the field yet (it's committed... think floating above), it is not a valid target for g1. After G1 finishes, the committed card will land.

Spidz 02/12/2025 8:05
a lot of card games do that though, where notionally 'cards effects are for when they're in play, unless it seems to only make sense in the worlds where that isn't true' (snapping snapper in lor, iirc, is like this). in addition, in the rules of the game, it says 'cards will break rules, the cards are right'
8:06
so both: other card games do that, and it does say in the rules that 'cards are right' as a catch all clause to remedy theoretical awkwardnesses like these

bobjoejuan
Can I flip Life 1, and then instantly flip Fire 0 twice to retrigger its effects? What happens to the bottom part of Fire 0
Michael Yang 02/12/2025 18:54
Life 1 can flip Fire 0 twice. The first flip makes it face-down, the second face-up. When it becomes face-up again, it's middle text triggers (it's not covered yet). Fire 0's bottom text doesn't retrigger (edited)

Gnarly Charlie 06/12/2025 2:00
So for example with that metal 6 card. If I were to flip it, would it first delete, then I resolve the middle text on the revealed card, and then flip that card?
2:01
Since the metal 6 first self deleted before I was able to flip it.
makario 06/12/2025 2:09
That scenario is tricky but the codex clarifies it:
When Plague 3's middle command triggers, the owner notes each uncovered face-up card. Then, they choose which card to process, one at a time. For each card, they flip it and process any consequences before addressing the next card.
The newly uncovered card gets resolved, but will not be flipped.
Gnarly Charlie 06/12/2025 2:17
Interesting, ok! Where can I find this codex you speak of?
makario 06/12/2025 2:18
See the pinned links

Khrono5 06/12/2025 5:49
almost, the card targeted for the flip of plague 3 was the metal 6, so the card under it gets to resolve its effect and does not get flipped afterwards (edited)

Michael Yang 08/12/2025 1:41

Sycopath
If I shift courage onto ice, does ice get to be shifted
Michael Yang 08/12/2025 1:46
If you do Courage 3's shift before Ice 3's top text, then Courage 3 could cover Ice 3 and allow it's text to happen.
1
Michael Yang
If you do Courage 3's shift before Ice 3's top text, then Courage 3 could cover Ice 3 and allow it's text to happen.
Khrono5 08/12/2025 3:03
thats surprising, I would have interpreted it as not being able to trigger because it wasn't satisfied when end step starts. But I guess the official statement is it just have to be face up at the start of the end step.
Michael Yang 08/12/2025 3:17
All your commands with End will activate during your End phase. When you collect these End triggers, no other conditions are being checked. Ice 3's condition of being covered is only addressed when you choose to process its End trigger. And, in this case, you're resolving Ice 3's End trigger after Courage 3's.
2
WebCobra 08/12/2025 3:43
Weird question but coming from an MTG background if i play life 1 and target light 0 to flip life 1 do I still resolve the second "flip 1 card"? Or once it's flipped it goes away
makario 08/12/2025 3:44
You do not. The second flip gets canceled.
WebCobra 08/12/2025 3:46

makario
You do not. The second flip gets canceled.
WebCobra 08/12/2025 3:47
That goes for all effects ya?
Michael Yang 08/12/2025 3:48
"Text that is active and is covered, flipped, or removed in some way is no longer active even if there was game text remaining" From the back of the Main 2 rules.
WebCobra
That goes for all effects ya?
makario 08/12/2025 3:50
Yep
Michael Yang
"Text that is active and is covered, flipped, or removed in some way is no longer active even if there was game text remaining" From the back of the Main 2 rules.

Ahh main 2 got it
Michael Yang 08/12/2025 3:54
I don't have every version of the rules but I believe it's been in every printing since the first. I just quoted it from the Main 2 one. Might even be the same exact text in all versions.

makario 12/12/2025 5:24
Whenever you have to draw and there is no deck, you shuffle your discard. (This is different from playing the top card of your deck, which does not shuffle.) I'll try to get a reference for you
5:24
For top-down play, you are not allowed to look before you place it on the field. You can look after, though
victhedestroyer 12/12/2025 5:25
Ok thanks. So any draw commands trigger a discard shuffle but top card plays do not?
makario 12/12/2025 5:26
Correct
victhedestroyer 12/12/2025 5:26
Assuming I have no cards in my deck*
makario 12/12/2025 5:27
Hm, I see in MN01 where you might think that it's refresh only:
Refresh: Draw cards from the top of your deck until you have 5 cards in hand. If you do not have enough cards in your deck to fill your hand, draw what you can then shuffle your trash to reform your deck and draw the remaining cards. You cannot refresh if you have 5 or more cards in your hand.
But later on, it talks about draw specifically:
Whenever you would draw cards and your deck is empty, shuffle your trash to form a new deck. Only drawing triggers this reshuffle — when your deck is empty, you cannot resolve non-drawing effects that involve the top card of your deck.
victhedestroyer 12/12/2025 5:31
I see, thanks. I realize where my misunderstanding was with regards to reshuffling. Thank you @makario
No Pepper 13/12/2025 3:36
So life 0, water 1, smoke 0
3:37
Don’t automatically play cards face down , if deck is empty or deck becomes empty ?
3:38
Rephrased: I need enough cards in my deck to accommodate “top deck play “?
Just Plain Mat 13/12/2025 3:45
correct
3:47
Your deck will only be "refreshed" with the cards from your trash as part of a draw action or a specific card effect like <clarity 2>.
Just Plain Mat

dellsouth 13/12/2025 23:58
Hi all, I have a question, if you play a card with a middle command, this command can affects to the same card which has been played or only affects to other cards?. Thanks in advance.
T̶ R̶ A̶ C̶ K̶ R̶ I̶ S̶ E̶ 14/12/2025 0:00
I believe the answer to your question is "it depends on the command". For example, a card with a middle command that says "Flip 1 card" can target itself. A card with a middle command of "Flip 1 other card" must target another card.

If it's not specified it can target itself

Michael Yang 14/12/2025 0:42
You can't refresh if you have 5 cards in hand. If you can't refresh, you can't use control with it

makario 14/12/2025 22:41

No Pepper 15/12/2025 1:36
Slick mechanic. Thanks for the help.
makario 15/12/2025 1:37
As written in the rules, first the compiling player deletes their cards, then the opponent deletes their cards.

makario 15/12/2025 1:37
Right.
TheBigBoy 15/12/2025 1:38

makario
As written in the rules, first the compiling player deletes their cards, then the opponent deletes their cards.

bobjoejuan
if the card you shift isn't covered, the text doesn't re-activate since it was never not visible in the first place
marshininja13 15/12/2025 5:19

5:21

Anton 15/12/2025 20:23
Suppose Hate 1 deletes a card that causes Water 0 to be revealed, which flips Hate 3 face up. Does the draw from Hate 3 trigger after the first delete (after all of its ramifications conclude) from Hate 1? What about after the second?
klenz 15/12/2025 20:39
you would get to draw 1 card, from the 2nd "delete 1 card" of the hate 1 card.
1
1
Michael Yang 16/12/2025 5:18
I believe you'd be able to draw 2 cards. The Hate 3 is face-up when you hit the "After you delete" window. There's no rule that says the "After" text needs to see the beginning AND end of an effect to trigger. Here's what happens: Hate 1 deletes a card, that activates Water 0, Water 0 flips Hate 3 face-up, Hate 3's top command is active, Water 0 flips itself, Hate 1's delete is finished, now we are "after you deleted a card" so Hate 3 activates and you draw a card, then Hate 1 does its second delete and you can draw a second card
1
Funny Samy 16/12/2025 5:51
Interesting, that's not how I would have thought it resolved but it makes sense
Eris 16/12/2025 8:49
I don't think I get it. "no rule that says the "After" text needs to see the begginning AND end of an effect to trigger" I get the first draw but when the second delete beggins Hate 3 is already face down isn't it? My idea was that when there's a separation by a dot then the next effect doesn't start until everything is resolved. So, in my mind, Hate 3 doesn't get to see the second delete
makario 16/12/2025 9:07
Hate 3 never gets flipped back down in this example
klenz 16/12/2025 11:59
so maybe my answer is actually wrong @Anton . In case of uncertainty, always trust Michael Yangs judgement on the rules, he knows a thing or two about this game
Anton 16/12/2025 12:08
Thank you - that makes sense!
makario
Hate 3 never gets flipped back down in this example
Eris 16/12/2025 13:51
Ooh I misred the example thx
13:52

Eris
In case of Hate 3 being face-up and then flipped face-down by Water 0, would you then draw 1 card?
dgbm 16/12/2025 14:48
No, because the first delete from Hate 1 triggers a series of effects that includes Hate 3 being flipped face down, so Hate 3's text is inactive before you reach the "after you delete" window. (or so I believe)
makario 16/12/2025 14:53
Right.
Eris 16/12/2025 15:03
So the "after x:" effect activates if the card is only face up at the end of an effect but it doesn't activate if the card is only face up at the begginning
makario 16/12/2025 15:05
Correct.
1

makario 25/12/2025 5:42

makario
Neither actually tell you to compile, which is important because it's not a compile action. You do not delete with diversity

DaMaddin 28/12/2025 10:14
Hey, so when active cards shift while still having pending text, do they keep the pending text or is that voided? For example I play a Life 1. It flips a face-down Gravity 1 face-up, with which I target the still active Life 1 to shift it on top of the Gravity 1. Do I get the second flip from Life 1 (for any other valid target, like any now uncovered card behind Life 1 after it was shifted)?
makario 28/12/2025 10:31
You do. Shifting does not cancel the card.
DaMaddin 28/12/2025 10:35

Impalsi 28/12/2025 20:56

Impalsi 28/12/2025 21:22
If two cards trigger simultaneously, does the player whose turn it is choose in which order to stack them?
Funny Samy 28/12/2025 21:29
Yes
21:29
Or rather it's whoever caused the simultaneous effects
21:29
I will note that some situations that seem simultaneous actually aren't, just out of curiosity what situation are you thinking of
Funny Samy
I will note that some situations that seem simultaneous actually aren't, just out of curiosity what situation are you thinking of
Impalsi 28/12/2025 21:32
Death 2 uncovering a card in each affected stack
Funny Samy 28/12/2025 21:36
Okay yeah that one is actually simultaneous
21:36
Yeah the player who owns the death 2 decides
Impalsi
Can Gravity 4 target a face down card that's already in its line
Michael Yang 28/12/2025 22:09
No. If you can change the game state because of an effect, you must. And if an effect can't change the game state, nothing happens.
Impalsi
Death 2 uncovering a card in each affected stack
Michael Yang 28/12/2025 22:15
You're right, the current player decides which card activates first. Note, that this is regardless of who actually triggered or owns the Death 2. If it's my turn, and I flip your face-down Death 2, you'd decide which line to delete cards in because it's your card's effect, but I would decide which of the uncovered middle commands happens first. If it's my turn, and I flip your Life 1 that you use to flip your face-down Death 2, then the same thing happens: you select the targets for your activated effects but I choose which of the simultaneous middle command activations happens first.
idk. 29/12/2025 2:09
If I steal a card from assimilation, and then they "return" that card, does it go back to them or am I the new owner?
makario 29/12/2025 2:10
You are the new owner

Michael Yang
You're right, the current player decides which card activates first. Note, that this is regardless of who actually triggered or owns the Death 2. If it's my turn, and I flip your face-down Death 2, you'd decide which line to delete cards in because it's your card's effect, but I would decide which of the uncovered middle commands happens first. If it's my turn, and I flip your Life 1 that you use to flip your face-down Death 2, then the same thing happens: you select the targets for your activated effects but I choose which of the simultaneous middle command activations happens first.

I'm trying to understand some things about shifted/committed cards and order of operations. Line 2 Speed[0], Spirit[3] Play Line 1 Light[2] Draw 2 cards Spirit[3] shifts to Line 1 revealing: Speed[0] (Play 1 card) Play Speed[3] (Shift 1 of your other cards) in Line 2. What is/are my valid targets to shift? (edited)
Funny Samy 30/12/2025 0:25
the spirit 3 is committed (so not yet on the field), your only target is light 2 i believe
0:25
unless there's something in the third line
Austin (Puma) 30/12/2025 0:29
Nothing in line three. So I can target the Light[2] since Spirit[3] is committed but not covering the Light card yet? Is Light[2] considered an uncovered card at this point in the logic?
Funny Samy 30/12/2025 0:30
yes
Austin (Puma) 30/12/2025 1:08
Making sure I understand this. In the codex it says: "When cards move between zones (e.g. hand/deck to the field for a play, moving from one line to another for a shift, or field to the trash from a delete), they first leave their current zone and get “committed” to the new zone" Which would mean that playing a card from hand causes the played card to enter the "committed" state before covering a card in its destination line. Which would also mean that if I played Chaos[2] in a line with a single card in it, I could not shift the card underneath Chaos[2]
Funmaker 30/12/2025 1:11
I don’t think that’s how it works in this case iirc
1:11
I think a card only becomes visible when it actually is on the field
1:12
Committing only triggers “when this would be covered”
1:12
Meaning the middle command only triggers once it’s on the field
Austin (Puma) 30/12/2025 1:12
ooooooh, okay that makes sense
Funny Samy 30/12/2025 1:22
Middle text of a card happens after it leaves the committed state

SoulSurvivor 01/01/2026 7:50
What is the design intention of the top effect of Fear 0? Is it supposed to be a downside or what? I've only seen it affect Mirror 1 when they're played together.
Funny Samy 01/01/2026 8:03
I see it as helping death/hate stuff
8:03
you can remove without worrying about revealing middle text that would help out the opponent
1
makario 01/01/2026 8:03
Often folks will defend against actions like deletes and shifts from their opponent by covering their powerful cards so that they will trigger if the opponent tries to remove or manipulate them.
8:05
Also, I don't believe <mirror 1> is affected by it
makario
Also, I don't believe <mirror 1> is affected by it
CompileBot BOT 01/01/2026 8:05
CompileBot v1.0.2
Funny Samy 01/01/2026 8:06
it is if you own the fear card
makario 01/01/2026 8:06
Mirror 1 happens on the end step of the active player, while Fear 0 prevents triggers from the active player's opponent
Funny Samy 01/01/2026 8:07
makario 01/01/2026 8:07
Ah, right! That case
8:08
Yeah, that would be...a negative synergy
Funny Samy
I see it as helping death/hate stuff
Michael Yang 01/01/2026 19:40
It also has internal synergy with Fear itself. You can use Fear 2 to return an opponents card without activating their uncovered effect
Alex Brogan 01/01/2026 21:15

Alex Brogan

SoulSurvivor
What is the design intention of the top effect of Fear 0? Is it supposed to be a downside or what? I've only seen it affect Mirror 1 when they're played together.
Alex Brogan 01/01/2026 21:16

21:17

Michael Yang
You're right, the current player decides which card activates first. Note, that this is regardless of who actually triggered or owns the Death 2. If it's my turn, and I flip your face-down Death 2, you'd decide which line to delete cards in because it's your card's effect, but I would decide which of the uncovered middle commands happens first. dgbm 04/01/2026 15:43
I play Speed 0 in Line 1, and use its middle command to play Speed 3 also in Line 1; in the End step, I shift Speed 3 to Line 2, uncovering Speed 0, and with its middle command, I commit another card to Line 2.
15:44
Because Speed 3 was commited first, it enters Line 2, followed by the card played with Speed 0, correct?
makario 04/01/2026 15:44
Yes. This also prevents S3 from flipping.

makario
Yes. This also prevents S3 from flipping.

Handy 04/01/2026 20:16
Hi everyone, new player here! Quick question because I'm not sure I'm reading the rules correctly and it seems quite powerful: If I have control and would compile at the start of my turn, I can rearrange my protocols before actually executing the compilation, correct? Essentially giving me free choice which of my protocols to compile? After reading the rules and the codex I'm pretty sure that's how it works, just wanted to make sure I'm not misinterpreting such an essential thing.
makario 04/01/2026 20:16
You're correct, it's before. Also, if you are refreshing and have to give up control, it is also before. You must rearrange before you draw back up to 5.
makario
You're correct, it's before. Also, if you are refreshing and have to give up control, it is also before. You must rearrange before you draw back up to 5.
Handy 04/01/2026 21:49
Understood, thank you! So in other words I will only know what cards I draw after rearranging.
Handy
Understood, thank you! So in other words I will only know what cards I draw after rearranging.
makario 04/01/2026 21:50
Correct.

Funmaker 07/01/2026 14:15
If I have <life 3> face up in a line covered by card A. Then I shift card B into that line, committing it. Before actually putting card B there I shift card A into another line. Now, does life 3 trigger?
Funmaker
If I have <life 3> face up in a line covered by card A. Then I shift card B into that line, committing it. Before actually putting card B there I shift card A into another line. Now, does life 3 trigger?
CompileBot BOT 07/01/2026 14:15
CompileBot v1.0.2
makario 07/01/2026 14:31
Yes, I believe so. The "would be covered" wouldn't trigger until B begins to land which happens after whatever triggered A to shift resolves
2
Funmaker 07/01/2026 16:02
Playing a solo game, I just realized the deck was empty anyways
Funny Samy 07/01/2026 16:05
Lol
Khrono5 07/01/2026 18:11
I think something interesting to think about is what order it would happen, does it happen before or after A's shift is complete. it ultimately probably doesn't matter, I see this scenario probably as B commits shift to revealing a card that allows you to shift A off life 3. So commit A to its new line making Life 3 revealed, would that immediately trigger life 3 then or would A land on its new line first(finishing resolving its shift) and then life 3 would then trigger right before B lands.
makario 07/01/2026 18:13
A would land first, to fully resolve the revealed card. B would not "jump the line"
18:15
Re: Life 3 triggering immediately on reveal, Life 3 does not have a middle trigger so nothing new happens. (edited)
18:15
Only middle commands trigger on reveal
Funny Samy 07/01/2026 18:20
I think it would help if the specific scenario were fleshed out more
makario
A would land first, to fully resolve the revealed card. B would not "jump the line"
Khrono5 07/01/2026 20:18
no disrespect and it might be my fault for not being clear enough but this is not what I was describing at all. I was referring to life 3s when covered effect interrupting the chain, not the order in which card A and B finish their shifts.
makario 07/01/2026 20:21
None taken. I guess my point is that "when covered" does not interrupt, in fact cannot interrupt, because it's not an immediate trigger. In order for it to trigger at all, B has to "finish" its shift in and land, which only happens after A has finished its shift and landed. (edited)
Khrono5 07/01/2026 20:23
so then are you saying that in this instance life 3 would not trigger at all, and that it would be covered before it got a chance to activate?
20:23
or that life 3 would trigger after A has landed but before B does.
makario 07/01/2026 20:25
No, it triggers. Let me back up:
So commit A to its new line making Life 3 revealed, would that immediately trigger life 3
No, because the fact that life 3 is revealed does not immediately trigger life 3. Revelation only triggers middle commands.
or would A land on its new line first(finishing resolving its shift) and then life 3 would then trigger right before B lands.
This is how it'd work (edited)
Khrono5 07/01/2026 20:32
okay thats more or less what I figured, the main significance would be if the card played off life 3 is in a completely different line than where A went, then A would be a valid target for the new cards effect mid chain, as opposed to if it was not landed it would not be a valid target for the new card. But given how when covered effects tend to trigger after revealed effects resolve this makes the most sense.
LightningRepairs 07/01/2026 23:14

makario 07/01/2026 23:15
It's 0=0, so no
23:15
Base is 0 not null
LightningRepairs 07/01/2026 23:15

Beginner here: if i play fire 0 and flips over fire 5, do I draw 2 first or do I discard 1 first?
makario 18/01/2026 3:53
Discard. The revealed 5 triggers immediately, then your return to what you were doing.
makario
Discard. The revealed 5 triggers immediately, then your return to what you were doing.
Funny Samy 18/01/2026 3:54
^
Cosmic 18/01/2026 4:07
Does Rearrange mean swap two protocols? Or all 3? Also is Water 2 rearrange protolcols a must and not a may?
Cosmic
Does Rearrange mean swap two protocols? Or all 3? Also is Water 2 rearrange protolcols a must and not a may?
makario 18/01/2026 4:08
Rearrange can be 2 or 3, but something must change. Swap (Spirit 4) is just 2. Water 2 is a must.
Cosmic 18/01/2026 4:08
Thanks
Cosmic 18/01/2026 4:22
Is Metal 3 optional? If there is a line with 8 or more cards must I delete that line?
makario 18/01/2026 4:23
Not optional. You've gotta do it, even if it explodes your own hard work
4:23
In general, if something is optional, it will say "may" like <spirit 2>. If it doesn't, you must do it.
makario
In general, if something is optional, it will say "may" like <spirit 2>. If it doesn't, you must do it.
CompileBot BOT 18/01/2026 4:23
CompileBot v1.0.2
Austin (Puma) 18/01/2026 12:59
Fire 0 and Speed 0 are in line 1. Fire 0 is shifted to line 2. Speed 0's middle effect triggers playing a card in line 2. Does Fire 0's bottom ability trigger?
henri 18/01/2026 13:13
No, because in the moment you activate speed 0, the fire 0 is committed to line 2 and in fact would not be covered, because it only actually is placed in the stack once the newly played card's effects are resolved and then the fire 0 is placed on top of the new card. (edited)
henri
No, because in the moment you activate speed 0, the fire 0 is committed to line 2 and in fact would not be covered, because it only actually is placed in the stack once the newly played card's effects are resolved and then the fire 0 is placed on top of the new card. (edited)
Austin (Puma) 18/01/2026 13:19
So fire 0 goes on top of the new card in line 2?
13:20
Shouldn't the newly played card in line 2 enter the 'committed' stack on top of Fire 0 since it's a card moving from one zone to another? (Hand > line 2) (edited)
Draconum 18/01/2026 14:28
If i flip <metal 6> with <light 0>, do i still draw 6 cards or does it get canceled because Metal 6 gets removed when targeted for a flip? Also what if I select any face-down card for flipping - is its value always 2 at the moment of flipping, hence I draw 2 cards? (edited)
Draconum
If i flip <metal 6> with <light 0>, do i still draw 6 cards or does it get canceled because Metal 6 gets removed when targeted for a flip? Also what if I select any face-down card for flipping - is its value always 2 at the moment of flipping, hence I draw 2 cards? (edited)
CompileBot BOT 18/01/2026 14:28
CompileBot v1.0.2
Draconum
If i flip <metal 6> with <light 0>, do i still draw 6 cards or does it get canceled because Metal 6 gets removed when targeted for a flip? Also what if I select any face-down card for flipping - is its value always 2 at the moment of flipping, hence I draw 2 cards? (edited)
Eris 18/01/2026 14:56
I'm not sure about the first one, but I believe the second question is the other way around. The card drawing part resolves when the card has already been flipped, so you'll draw cards equal to the face up value. If you use it to flip a face-up card, you'll always draw 2 cards.
Draconum
If i flip <metal 6> with <light 0>, do i still draw 6 cards or does it get canceled because Metal 6 gets removed when targeted for a flip? Also what if I select any face-down card for flipping - is its value always 2 at the moment of flipping, hence I draw 2 cards? (edited)
makario 18/01/2026 14:57
You'll draw 6. That card will land in the trash, which is always face up. See the codex for more
makario
You'll draw 6. That card will land in the trash, which is always face up. See the codex for more
Draconum 18/01/2026 14:58
okay thanks - and so the card value before flipping is the one that decides the quantity for drawing right?
makario 18/01/2026 15:01
It mentions "that card" so the card itself does not change. The value is the value of the card at the time the second sentence resolves. For flipping face down, it will usually be 2. For flipping face up, it will be the value of the face up card. Metal 6 is just tricky because it ends up in the trash, but it's still face up there
Austin (Puma)
Shouldn't the newly played card in line 2 enter the 'committed' stack on top of Fire 0 since it's a card moving from one zone to another? (Hand > line 2) (edited)
makario 18/01/2026 15:01
Fire 0 does indeed trigger

Austin (Puma) 18/01/2026 15:02

1
makario 18/01/2026 15:02
You'll shift it to line 2, play a card on top of it. Cards enter the line in the order they are queued. Fire 0 is on the field and will trigger when the second card enters
15:03
Yep (edited)

Austin (Puma)

makario 18/01/2026 15:04

15:05

15:07
lemme grab an example in asec
Austin (Puma) 18/01/2026 15:19
makario 18/01/2026 15:23
Yeah, nothing has been written out in stone for this particular example. I was randomly talking with Michael the other day about this very thing. I believe the speed 0 will fully resolve first, effectively cancelling out the second flip, but to be clear this is not codified yet
makario
Yeah, nothing has been written out in stone for this particular example. I was randomly talking with Michael the other day about this very thing. I believe the speed 0 will fully resolve first, effectively cancelling out the second flip, but to be clear this is not codified yet
Austin (Puma) 18/01/2026 15:28
Ah gotcha. Speed 0 resolving first avoids some issues, but then I think committed card resolution time becomes inconsistent
makario 18/01/2026 15:28
How so?
Austin (Puma) 18/01/2026 15:30
Id have to find an exact example but my general understanding is the whole intent behind committed cards as rule is that they resolve at the end of all middle card commands concluding their actions to avoid infinite loops
15:33
It seems it would just be an exception to speed 0. I suppose a way to keep logic consistent is that cards played from your hand don't enter the committed queue (which admittedly is how my group has been playing not understanding the rule)
makario 18/01/2026 15:33
Would definitely love more examples. I'm particularly interested in this at the moment. But re: your last part, I think the intent is to resolve after current triggers, yes, but not necessarily all, particularly not necessarily for triggers that occurred prior (if that makes sense) (edited)
Austin (Puma) 18/01/2026 15:36
Yeah I put some more stuff together later when I have time. I think defining the line at what "current triggers" are is very difficult to parse and perhaps subjective. Even if you do find that line, the intent I assume is to avoid game breaking states. And so where you draw that line may rely moreso on what prevents the infinite
makario 18/01/2026 15:38
Right, sure. I think the ultimate goal is to make it less subjective while still being easy to parse. A challenge! (FWIW, the infinite still gets prevented either way)
15:38
Feel free to dm me more examples at any time, I'm collecting them

henri 18/01/2026 16:01
I just learned cards played, not just the ones shifted, also always become committed before entering, which changes a lot of card interactions. No clue how i missed that before. Thanks for the clarification.
1
henri 18/01/2026 17:31
After much consideration, i find that kind of unintuitive, and in complex situations hard to keep track of: The way a shifted card enters play is the same as a played card, but the way it executes is not based on the way it enters, but instead on the reason why it enters, which is not indicated in any way. You have to memorize how cards got into the committed position to know whether they trigger or not. Under my previous understanding, only shifted cards becoming committed to a line (while their uncovered effect and any further triggered effects resolve), committed cards never trigger their middle effect upon entering the stack. It being the way i now understand it to be makes some lines of play less complex, but in general makes shifting less intuitively understandable and relies on memory for long chains of effects. Can you think of any example, why it needs to be this way?
Funny Samy 18/01/2026 17:32
Wait
henri
After much consideration, i find that kind of unintuitive, and in complex situations hard to keep track of: The way a shifted card enters play is the same as a played card, but the way it executes is not based on the way it enters, but instead on the reason why it enters, which is not indicated in any way. You have to memorize how cards got into the committed position to know whether they trigger or not. Under my previous understanding, only shifted cards becoming committed to a line (while their uncovered effect and any further triggered effects resolve), committed cards never trigger their middle effect upon entering the stack. It being the way i now understand it to be makes some lines of play less complex, but in general makes shifting less intuitively understandable and relies on memory for long chains of effects. Can you think of any example, why it needs to be this way?
makario 18/01/2026 17:33
In what ways are they different?
Funny Samy 18/01/2026 17:33
Even if you only look at shifting, it's not true that committed cards never trigger their middle effect upon entering the stack
17:34
E.g. when you move a covered card, since the middle text goes from being obscured to being visible it will trigger
17:34
So the context is always important in that sense
Funny Samy
So the context is always important in that sense
henri 18/01/2026 17:35
i forgot about darkness 0. hm.
henri
After much consideration, i find that kind of unintuitive, and in complex situations hard to keep track of: The way a shifted card enters play is the same as a played card, but the way it executes is not based on the way it enters, but instead on the reason why it enters, which is not indicated in any way. You have to memorize how cards got into the committed position to know whether they trigger or not. Under my previous understanding, only shifted cards becoming committed to a line (while their uncovered effect and any further triggered effects resolve), committed cards never trigger their middle effect upon entering the stack. It being the way i now understand it to be makes some lines of play less complex, but in general makes shifting less intuitively understandable and relies on memory for long chains of effects. Can you think of any example, why it needs to be this way?
Austin (Puma) 18/01/2026 18:46
Oh yeah. I had never noticed that
makario
In what ways are they different?
henri 18/01/2026 18:48
example: play speed 0 from hand. -> play speed 3 ontop of it -> resolve speed 3 in any way that doesn't cover speed 3. End: Speed 3 shifts itself into another line, becoming committed while speed 0 activates and i play a card into the same line in which speed 3 is committed. Under my previous understanding, the card i now play would enter, trigger and then be covered by speed 3, which then flips itself, ending up ontop of my played card. Under the understanding i now gained, the card would become committed, speed three enters first, then my newly played card enters, covering speed three, triggering its effect and preventing speed 3's effect from flipping it. (edited)
KarlBirdsong 18/01/2026 18:51
Just got main 2 to the table, can mirror 3 flip any card, covered or not?
makario 18/01/2026 18:52
No. Only non-covered.
18:53
(If the card does not say all, or refer to a specific card using the words "this" or "that" then only top cards are targetable)
KarlBirdsong 18/01/2026 18:53
Thanks for the quick response!

Michael Yang 19/01/2026 2:11
I read through the conversation and wrote up responses for all of Austin's questions (I think). Thanks for the questions Austin! I'm going to put all the answers below, quoting Austin's original post for each (good thing they were all separate messages).
Austin (Puma)
Fire 0 and Speed 0 are in line 1. Fire 0 is shifted to line 2. Speed 0's middle effect triggers playing a card in line 2. Does Fire 0's bottom ability trigger?
Michael Yang 19/01/2026 2:12
So, first, if not for any shenanigans, when a card shifts, you just pick it up and move it to the new stack. But, in this case, Speed 0 wants to start some shenanigans. We know that Speed 0 activates when I pick up Fire 0 to shift it. But I'm a lowly computer so I can only do one operation at a time. The rules tell me I must do Speed 0 now so I need to stop doing the Fire 0 shift first. To stop doing Fire 0, I need a holding space to place the card - I put it under the stack it will eventually enter to show that that's its intended home. Fire 0 becomes the head of the commit queue for that stack. Now Speed 0 can start. I choose a card and move it out of my hand. I go to put it in line 2, But wait! There's something in the way! Now I have to stop playing this card. I place this newly played card under the stack it's supposed to move into. There's already a card in the queue for that stack, I place the new card below it. Now that's done. I look back to the commit queue that was started first: the one for my side of line 2. I need to take the head of the queue and land it in the stack. I move Fire 0 from the commit queue to the stack. Now that's done, I repeat. I look back to the commit queue that was started first. It's the one with the Speed 0 card play. The played card tries to land, Fire 0 says WAIT! I have to happen first! So I need to stop landing the the played card. I put it below Fire 0. That's done so I can now do Fire 0. I do my flipping stuff. That's done. There are no more commit queues to process. The original shift of Fire 0 is done. We hit the "After a shift" window.
2
Austin (Puma)
Gotcha. So even if there was a card that said "Play a card. Draw 2 cards." The played card wouldn't trigger until after drawing 2 cards correct?
Michael Yang 19/01/2026 2:14
The played card's middle text activates before you draw 2 cards. This is because the card's middle text activating is part of the "play" action. Playing a card means: choose the card to play, it's orientation, and destination stack. Move the card to the stack. Activate its middle command. So, only when all of that is done do we hit the window "After a card is played". After those are done, we finally move to the Draw 2 cards part.
Austin (Puma)
Click to see attachment 🖼️
Michael Yang 19/01/2026 2:15
This is a great example of the ambiguity. I'll explain it as it is in my head. That said, the framework I'm operating under may not be ideal. Certainly, it could be explained better. The first flip caused all these cards to be committed. So, that flip isn't done until those cards leave the committed queue. Once those cards exit the commit queue, then we'd hit an "After flip" activation. There's none of that here so we move on to the next part of the middle command activation. I believe you already explained that you learned this in your 2nd and 3rd videos but wanted to write the answer for this here since it's a good question.
Austin (Puma)
Click to see attachment 🖼️
Michael Yang 19/01/2026 2:17
I think this video is at the core of the ambiguity. Luckily, I believe your conclusion matches mine. The '2' is blocked from landing so we go back to land the Fire 0. Now, how to explain this is the tricky part... Any thoughts appreciated! It's straight forward for me if I'm walking through an example but it's been difficult to codify in a robust way.
blakadder.com 19/01/2026 2:21
The commit stack usually resolves LIFO? Is that correct
Michael Yang 19/01/2026 2:23
Commit queue is FIFO. The rest of the game is LIFO.
blakadder.com 19/01/2026 2:25
And what do you mean by 2 landing being blocked? Isn't it supposed to wait for placement after the first thing on the stack (Fire 0) resolves?
Michael Yang 19/01/2026 2:29
The '2' wants to land but it can't because there's a committed card blocking it from entering. The '2' has to wait for the Fire 0 to land in the stack before it can land.
blakadder.com 19/01/2026 2:31
If f.e. Fire 0 was committed to a different line the 2 would land in it's stack before Fire 0 resolving?
Michael Yang 19/01/2026 2:37
I'm not following. Are you talking about Fire 0 middle command activating because of a play or uncover, or are you talking about Fire 0's bottom command activating because of being covered? (edited)
Michael Yang
Commit queue is FIFO. The rest of the game is LIFO.
Austin (Puma) 19/01/2026 2:40
I think this is one of the biggest things that's hard to wrap my head around the first time. Im pretty confident that I understand all the committed card stuff now. But there's two or three pieces of it that I've had to take time understanding one step at a time
Impalsi 19/01/2026 2:43
Water 3 triggers, targeting the Life/Gravity line. Spirit 5 is committed to the Gravity line. Gravity 2 is bounced by Water 3 Gravity 0 triggers How exactly does Gravity 0 resolve? Do its cards enter the committed queue? When they land, do they go under Gravity 0? (edited)
Michael Yang
I'm not following. Are you talking about Fire 0 middle command activating because of a play or uncover, or are you talking about Fire 0's bottom command activating because of being covered? (edited)
blakadder.com 19/01/2026 2:44
I'm talking about Fire 0 sitting in committed queue due to being moved. As I see it, it is waiting to be placed in it's new stack but waits to resolve triggers that happened due to it leaving
2:48
Is the committed card "reserving" a landing space in the stack and the stack cannot be continued until it fully lands?
Impalsi
Water 3 triggers, targeting the Life/Gravity line. Spirit 5 is committed to the Gravity line. Gravity 2 is bounced by Water 3 Gravity 0 triggers How exactly does Gravity 0 resolve? Do its cards enter the committed queue? When they land, do they go under Gravity 0? (edited)
Michael Yang 19/01/2026 2:53
Gravity 0's plays go into the committed queue like other played cards. The special thing for them is that instead of landing on top of the stack, they get put right under Gravity 0
blakadder.com
Is the committed card "reserving" a landing space in the stack and the stack cannot be continued until it fully lands?
Michael Yang 19/01/2026 2:54
I think this is right. I've never thought of the spot being "reserved" but that makes sense.
blakadder.com 19/01/2026 3:15
It's just my Magic the Gathering judge brain trying to name things in the game. I'm planning on trying to create a comprehensive rules webpage once I get some experience with Main 2
makoismycopilot 19/01/2026 3:18
What do y’all think the best how to play video for showing Compile to new folks is? Not overly detailed / bogged in the weeds, easy to understand, straight forward.
3:20

henri 19/01/2026 3:40
So essentially the committed cards are in a single file funnel being dispensed one at a time. About presentation: The picture in Austin's video, visually having multiple cards committed in a line without overlapping one another, like on a well organized conveyor belt flowing toward the stack—two dimensional and in each other's path—helped me a lot to grasp the functionality. That detail of not stacking the committed cards of one line ontop of or underneath each other helps a lot in instinctually understanding how they're supposed to flow. Kinda like packages with a barcode of their destination, flowing off a conveyor to that destination. That visual of address-stickered packages also immediately solves the question of whether the gravity 0 being covered when the committed face-down cards enter is still an active effect or not and whether they therefor flow in underneath it still, or default to another location now. Maybe a small pictogram can help with a definite explanation in the smallest space of how the committed queue works.
Michael Yang
I think this video is at the core of the ambiguity. Luckily, I believe your conclusion matches mine. The '2' is blocked from landing so we go back to land the Fire 0. Now, how to explain this is the tricky part... Any thoughts appreciated! It's straight forward for me if I'm walking through an example but it's been difficult to codify in a robust way.
henri 19/01/2026 3:46
"Under the stack" meaning in the space of the line for committed cards, closer to the player than the player's stack of that line, can rapidly become really ambiguous with cardtext using the same terminology of "under" to refer to placement as part of the stack.
blakadder.com 19/01/2026 3:48
Below the stack then?
henri 19/01/2026 4:03
I've been looking at synonyms for quite some time now... All of the single word solutions i can come up with are tied to elevation still. "Place the card below the stack." still has a high chance of being understood as "covered by the stack". I couldn't come up with a single word that is explicitly understood, without a definition being written down. All i can come up with is relative descriptions like the one above or such as "between the player and their stack in that line". :/

So, first, if not for any shenanigans, when a card shifts, you just pick it up and move it to the new stack. But, in this case, Speed 0 wants to start some shenanigans. We know that Speed 0 activates when I pick up Fire 0 to shift it. But I'm a lowly computer so I can only do one operation at a time. The rules tell me I must do Speed 0 now so I need to stop doing the Fire 0 shift first. To stop doing Fire 0, I need a holding space to place the card - I put it under the stack it will eventually enter to show that that's its intended home. Fire 0 becomes the head of the commit queue for that stack. Now Speed 0 can start. I choose a card and move it out of my hand. I go to put it in line 2, But wait! There's something in the way! Now I have to stop playing this card. I place this newly played card under the stack it's supposed to move into. There's already a card in the queue for that stack, I place the new card below it. Now that's done. I look back to the commit queue that was started first: the one for my side of line 2. I need to take the head of the queue and land it in the stack. I move Fire 0 from the commit queue to the stack. Now that's done, I repeat. I look back to the commit queue that was started first. It's the one with the Speed 0 card play. The played card tries to land, Fire 0 says WAIT! I have to happen first! So I need to stop landing the the played card. I put it below Fire 0. That's done so I can now do Fire 0. I do my flipping stuff. That's done. There are no more commit queues to process. The original shift of Fire 0 is done. We hit the "After a shift" window.

henri 20/01/2026 19:08
I had a really fun time, exploring the draft implications, if speed 0 speed 3 and psyche 1 would be a 3 card combo from hand which could lock your opponent out of playing face-up cards. (imagine only shifted cards become committed and cards played would enter lines before cards in the commited queue. Was our misunderstanding at the time.) Turned out it was actually very counterable in draft and perfectly fine to exist, balance wise, but that knowledge had to be there for both players. The resulting testing matches for this were some of the most fun compile matches i have had so far. My opponent and me had to lock in as the fallout of every single decision in the matches demanded very high levels of play, watching a dozen possible causalities to any one choice. (im still a tiny bit bummed about that being an incorrect rules understanding.)
19:11
I think my take-away is, that i like volatility as it intensifies the match.
19:15

blakadder.com 21/01/2026 10:38
@Michael Yang I entered rules and forum posts into NotebookLLM and asked to define commited: In the game of Compile, a committed card is one that is currently in a transient state while moving between different game zones. The Committed State When a card moves—such as being played from your hand or deck to the field, shifting from one line to another, or being deleted and sent to the trash—it follows a specific sequence: 1. Departure: The card leaves its current zone. 2. Commitment: It enters a "committed" state. 3. Resolution: Any consequences of the card leaving its previous zone (such as uncovering another card and triggering its middle command) are resolved. 4. Landing: The committed card finally "lands" in its new zone. Key Rules for Committed Cards • Immunity to Manipulation: While a card is in the committed state, it cannot be targeted, selected, or manipulated by any other game effects. It also maintains its orientation and its intended target zone throughout the move. • Guaranteed Entry: Nothing can prevent a committed card from eventually entering the field in the zone it was committed to. • Interaction with "Would be Covered" effects: If a committed card is about to cover a card with a "When this card would be covered: first..." trigger, that effect (and any resulting triggers) must resolve completely before the committed card enters the line. During this resolution, the committed card is not a valid selection for any player who gains the ability to manipulate cards. • Queuing: It is possible for multiple cards to be committed to a single line at once. In these instances, they enter the line one at a time, in the specific order they were committed. First card commited is the first one that enters the line (FIFO order). In summary, the committed state serves as a mechanical "waiting room" that ensures card movements are completed in an orderly fashion and are protected from outside interference until they have fully landed in their new position

blakadder.com 21/01/2026 13:27
According to the sources, the term manipulated encompasses several specific actions: • Flip: Changing a card from face-down to face-up or vice-versa. • Delete: Moving a card from the field to the owner's trash. • Return: Moving a card from the field back to its owner’s hand. • Shift: Moving a card to another line on the same side of the field. • Reveal: Publicly sharing information about a card (like showing both players a face-down card) before returning it to its previous state.
