henri 24/01/2026 18:32
So, looking over the codex(16DEC2024) and rereading the section on committed cards thoroughly, how i've been explained here in more detail varies from what is written there. - Cards being committed to hand, deck, or trash is not the case as per codex. „Cards entering play by being played from hand, top of deck, trash, or any other place as well as cards being shifted are considered “committed”.“ This defines explicit cases, none of which are for example cards being returned or deleted. The further sentences hone in on lines being the only destination for which this is the case. Other destinations are never mentioned. It would be nice if the phrasing would be changed to include those. For example something like *»Cards moving between hands, decks, trashs, and lines become "committed" while during or by that movement an effect is triggered. The triggered effect is resolved fully first, including any further triggered effects nested therein.« - Oder of committed cards across multiple lines exiting committed state is not defined in the codex. The closest mention to go off would be „More than one card can be committed to a line at a time. They will enter the line in the order they were committed, one at a time.“ Say first a card is committed to line A, which triggers an effect, committing a card to line B. Which of the cards enters its line first is entirely undefined in the codex. My current understanding is, that each committed queue within itself is FIFO, while the rest of the game, even priority between committed queues is LIFO. Can the codex include this information? Edit: Latest codex answers both questions. (edited)
1

I know that in the rules, there is a mention that unless otherwise stated, we as players can choose who it targets. Does that hold true tho? For example, with <Darkness 4> – “shift 1 face-down card”: can that card be mine or my opponent’s, or only mine? More generally, for effects like “Shift”, “Discard”, “Flip”, or “Delete”, when is the target restricted to my own cards only, and when can I legally choose an opponent’s card as the target? Does each effect need explicit wording to allow targeting the opponent? A short clarification or example would help a lot. And sorry if the answer is obvious, it has been quite a day today
Laenitus

klenz 27/01/2026 10:07

Alex Brogan 27/01/2026 20:45
A good way to think about this in general would be to categorize effects as “field” effects and “personal” effects. (edited)
20:47
Field effects can target any uncovered card on the field as long as that card fits the parameters/prerequisites of the effect and/or can target any covered card so long as the effect specifies “covered” cards as targets. If “all” cards are targeted, this includes covered cards. (edited)
20:49
Personal effects include things like “draw”, “discard”, and “shuffle”. Personal effects are always acted out by the player being targeted and that player must follow all directions/limitations given by the effect. (edited)
20:50
If an effect says “your opponent discards a card” that is a personal effect acted out by your opponent, so they choose the card that is discarded. If it says “draw the drop card of your opponent’s deck” you are the one drawing and the directions given by the effect require you to draw from their deck rather than your own so you must do it.
20:51
I don’t think any keywords fall outside of these two categories.
20:53
Not a rules thing, but an idea for future Mains based on this explanation… codify these categories and then you can have a command like “Ignore all field effects. Start: Flip this card.” (edited)
Laenitus 27/01/2026 21:15
Great explaining Alex!
21:19

makario 31/01/2026 21:44
Light 0 lands first. Then resolves, without Spirit 3 being targetable. Then, after it (and Speed 0) finish resolving, Spirit 3 lands.

Prylore 01/02/2026 4:02
Hi, I never really talk here but, if a card with an end step trigger is revealed, can that cards trigger still happen or has it missed timing?
Prylore
Hi, I never really talk here but, if a card with an end step trigger is revealed, can that cards trigger still happen or has it missed timing?
Michael Yang 01/02/2026 4:04
All End: and Start: text activates at the very beginning of their respective phases and only then. If they get revealed or played during the phase, they don't activate.
Michael Yang
All End: and Start: text activates at the very beginning of their respective phases and only then. If they get revealed or played during the phase, they don't activate.
Prylore 01/02/2026 4:04
Alright! Thank you very much
Impalsi
Which queue resolves now? Line 2 or 3's?
Michael Yang 01/02/2026 4:12
I believe this answers your specific question. The important bit is that Light 0 lands before the Spirit 3. The change from LIFO is only relevant when processing a queue itself; in that case, you process a queue FIFO. (edited)
4:15
I'm happy to hop on a call and talk through all of this if you'd like.
Michael Yang
I believe this answers your specific question. The important bit is that Light 0 lands before the Spirit 3. The change from LIFO is only relevant when processing a queue itself; in that case, you process a queue FIFO. (edited)
Impalsi 01/02/2026 4:26
Thanks for taking the time. Interesting that Life 3 is considered "done" despite its card still being in queue. By that I mean - we have to resolve all the effects of playing a card in C before Light 0 can land. Enqueueing the card from Life 3 is sufficient to count as "resolved" in this case, but for the purposes of Speed 0's resolution, enqueueing the card is not sufficient
Impalsi
Thanks for taking the time. Interesting that Life 3 is considered "done" despite its card still being in queue. By that I mean - we have to resolve all the effects of playing a card in C before Light 0 can land. Enqueueing the card from Life 3 is sufficient to count as "resolved" in this case, but for the purposes of Speed 0's resolution, enqueueing the card is not sufficient
Michael Yang 01/02/2026 4:35
I didn't go into breaking up the Play action into it's different steps: decision, commit, land, and middle command activation. I have my previous example I shared in this channel of a similar situation that is broken up more explicitly if we need something with more detail. But, for this example, when I say "done", it's shorthand for "Life 3 is done with what it can currently do". "Done" in this case isn't the same as hitting the window "After a card is played"; that would happen after the played card lands and does its middle command. So, for the effects of playing Life 3's card in step 4, we were able to make the decision and commit the card. The landing was blocked because there's another card already in the queue. At that point, Life 3 has completed all it can and we move backwards to the last thing that triggered the Life 3 - LIFO (edited)
1
Michael Yang
I didn't go into breaking up the Play action into it's different steps: decision, commit, land, and middle command activation. I have my previous example I shared in this channel of a similar situation that is broken up more explicitly if we need something with more detail. But, for this example, when I say "done", it's shorthand for "Life 3 is done with what it can currently do". "Done" in this case isn't the same as hitting the window "After a card is played"; that would happen after the played card lands and does its middle command. So, for the effects of playing Life 3's card in step 4, we were able to make the decision and commit the card. The landing was blocked because there's another card already in the queue. At that point, Life 3 has completed all it can and we move backwards to the last thing that triggered the Life 3 - LIFO (edited)

"Done" in this case isn't the same as hitting the window "After a card is played"; that would happen after the played card lands and does its middle command. So, for the effects of playing Life 3's card in step 4, we were able to make the decision and commit the card. The landing was blocked because there's another card already in the queue. bobjoejuan
if you return a card to an opponent's empty hand, revealing a Discard a card effect, does that card get discarded?
Michael Yang 08/02/2026 0:19
Right, the revealed middle command happens first, before the returning card lands in the hand
2
BlueMoons 08/02/2026 1:17
here's a fun one,
Processing Multiple Cards Triggering Simultaneously When there are multiple cards that trigger at the same time (e.g. Two cards in play each have a command with “After a card is discarded:” and a card is discarded), the current player decides the order in which the cards are processed.
if i've got 3 start effects, i assume i don't get to decide to resolve one first and then resolve the second and third based on the results of the first? i doubt there's been any time where i've had to stack more than two triggers where the first changed the amount of information i had, but something like it could happen with Clarity
makario 08/02/2026 1:51
I'm not sure I understand your question, but if I have an empty hand and have Courage 0, Clarity 1, Corruption 2 and Plague 1 on the field, I definitely would choose to activate Courage 0 BEFORE activating Clarity 1. (This is quite the contrived example...)
1:52
Because yes, you do get to choose the order of resolution, and by the time you activate the other starts, the board state may have changed.
1:53
Perhaps a more straightforward example is to choose to activate Death 1 (or Courage 0) before Spirit 1 so you have something to discard
BlueMoons 08/02/2026 2:02
in magic you have to stack all your trigger before you start resolving
2:02

makario 08/02/2026 2:03
The latter
BlueMoons 08/02/2026 2:03
in practice, this will basically never matter
2:03
really? you'd go with the latter? with all the stack-based gameplay in compile i'd expect you to put them on the stack properly beforehand.
makario 08/02/2026 2:05

2:05

yeah i'm talking specifically situations where you have 3+ things legitimately triggering simultaneously

makario 08/02/2026 2:17
I do suppose the Codex could clarify it. I wonder if the closest Codex example would be the clarification for Plague 3, on noting and choosing:
When Plague 3's middle command triggers, the owner notes each uncovered face-up card. Then, they choose which card to process, one at a time. For each card, they flip it and process any consequences before addressing the next card.
BlueMoons
here's a fun one,
Processing Multiple Cards Triggering Simultaneously When there are multiple cards that trigger at the same time (e.g. Two cards in play each have a command with “After a card is discarded:” and a card is discarded), the current player decides the order in which the cards are processed.
if i've got 3 start effects, i assume i don't get to decide to resolve one first and then resolve the second and third based on the results of the first? i doubt there's been any time where i've had to stack more than two triggers where the first changed the amount of information i had, but something like it could happen with Clarity
Michael Yang 08/02/2026 2:54
I always choose these triggers one at a time. End phase starts, I collect End triggers, I choose one End trigger from the collection to resolve, I choose another. I'll rewrite the Codex and rules sheet to explain this more clearly - good find.
Impalsi 08/02/2026 3:35
Suppose I shift a facedown card from Line A to Line B. Doing so uncovers Light 0 in Line A. In Line B, there is Life 3. So shifting has at least two results: triggering Light 0 and triggering Life 3. Do those happen simultaneously? And thus I'd get to order them as I please? Or does one happen before the other?
Michael Yang 08/02/2026 3:47
These don't happen simultaneously. Uncovering Light 0 interrupts the shift and happens first.
BlueMoons
in magic you have to stack all your trigger before you start resolving
Khrono5 08/02/2026 7:25
I had this same issue when I started the game too, but as others have explained you just note the simultaneous effects being triggered and process each one in separate chains. It is very different from traditional TCGs where you would activate them all on the same chain.

BlueMoons 08/02/2026 15:46
The shift commits the card you moved to the new line. middle commands get uncovered while this shift is in progress, it's the whole reason committing is a thing. because the uncovered middle command happens during a time while the card is in transit you can't loop stuff infinitely.
15:47
at the end of the shift, the committed card lands in the new line, and the trigger of a would be covered clause happens then.
Impalsi
Is this written somewhere? I never got that impression in all my reading of the rules and the codex. In my head canon, they both happen while the card is committed so they are simultaneous If it's covered somewhere in the codex I'd love to go read it If not, it could be a helpful thing to add Thanks for getting back to me
makario 08/02/2026 15:55
When the consequences of them leaving their current zone are done (e.g. uncovering a card and triggering its middle command), the committed card lands in the new zone.
I guess the part that isn't stated is that the "onCover" trigger occurs on the land step, as BlueMoons pointed out
Impalsi 08/02/2026 15:57
So "would be covered" effects don't happen while the covering card is committed?
makario 08/02/2026 15:58
If the committed card will cover a card with a “When this card would be covered: first…” text, the soon-to-be-covered card resolves its triggered effect and any other resulting triggers before the committed card enters the line. If, through the triggering of that “When this card would be covered: first…” effect, either player gains the ability to manipulate a card, the committed card IS NOT a valid selection.
15:58
It's still committed; it hasn't landed yet
Impalsi 08/02/2026 15:59
Okay, so I'd argue there's a hidden step happening then. Because to me it seems that triggering the on-covered effects is one of the consequences of the card leaving its current zone
15:59

makario 08/02/2026 16:00

Impalsi 08/02/2026 16:01

16:01

16:03

16:03

makario 08/02/2026 16:05
Mhm (shift > reveal > queue > begin-land > cover) > land > trigger is quite a lot of steps (edited)
1
Michael Yang 08/02/2026 18:32
I think of a shift as having 2 parts: leaving and landing. Leaving is where you pick up the cards, landing is when you put the card down in its final resting place. The leaving step can result in a reveal which necessitates a commit. The landing step can result in an onCover which also necessitates a commit.

Chris Williams 09/02/2026 21:48
If I have 0 cards in my hand at the end of my turn, would both Unity 4 and Peace 1 trigger? Or would I have to pick one of them since once one of them triggers I will then have cards in my hand?
Funny Samy 09/02/2026 21:50
My understanding is that you have to decide the order in which these trigger
21:50
Then the first one would work but then when you get to the second one it checks your hand and sees the cards so it fails
1
Chris Williams 09/02/2026 21:58
That's how I played it but I wasn't sure if the check for an empty hand for both was prior to anything resolving.
EEH 09/02/2026 22:01
I thought they both would trigger, in the order you choose. I was under the impression that eligibility is checked simultaneously (at the end of your turn, in this case), both triggers enter the 'stack' (to borrow the MTG term), and then you can execute them, even if the eligibility changed during their execution.
Michael Yang 09/02/2026 22:08
Both End's activate one at a time, in the player's chosen order. When you activate the End trigger, you do what the text says. So the first one resolves, it asks you to check your hand size, and then does a thing. The second one resolves, it checks your hand size, and then nothing else happens because your hand isn't empty. The only thing making them "eligible" to trigger is the End: text. So, even in the case where you're hand is full when you hit your End step, both cards activate, they just then do nothing.
Michael Yang
Both End's activate one at a time, in the player's chosen order. When you activate the End trigger, you do what the text says. So the first one resolves, it asks you to check your hand size, and then does a thing. The second one resolves, it checks your hand size, and then nothing else happens because your hand isn't empty. The only thing making them "eligible" to trigger is the End: text. So, even in the case where you're hand is full when you hit your End step, both cards activate, they just then do nothing.
Chris Williams 09/02/2026 22:13
Thank you!

fruit 11/02/2026 3:47
I have a question regarding Reveals, specificially hand reveals as in Clarity 1, Light 4 and Psychic 0. The codex states that "Reveal means to show both players in the game whatever information is being revealed, then return it to its previous state", which would imply that once someone has studied their opponents hand it has to be turned around again. However the unofficial Compile game vs AI online handles it differently. Instead the already revealed hand stays revealed until the cards leave said hand. New cards added to the hand are not revealed. I found a thread on this server that mentioned that this had been added as a feature because otherwise the time to look at the AIs hand would have been to short. So I guess my question is: How would you handle these effects and what is the official ruling? Personally I am a big fan of leaving the cards in a revealed state as long as they are on that hand. (edited)
makario 11/02/2026 3:50
Often digital games keep the revealed cards out for accessibility, however this doesn't work well with Compile's face-down mechanic. Imagine you reveal your hand, but then want to play a card face down. If the revealed cards stayed revealed, your opponent will know which card was played face down.
fruit 11/02/2026 3:51
I thought this would be one of the benefits of revealing the hand
makario 11/02/2026 3:52
I don't think so. As the codex mentions, you return to the original state; a subsequent face down play shouldn't be affected by that reveal. (And certainly not several turns later.)
fruit 11/02/2026 3:56
thx for the clarification. I think this makes revealing your opponents hand really challenging during games that have some sort of time control mechanic like a chess clock and so on. Unless you stop the time for that. Something to consider for the tournament I am currently planning
3:57
you could of course also argue that quickly remembering the opponents hand counts as a skill but I am not sure whether this would be fun
Impalsi 11/02/2026 3:58
i actually played a round of compile with a chess clock. we paused time during actions like that, shuffling, etc.. where a decision isn't being made
3:59
if you're doing it digitally, i'd suggest having players quickly screenshot the hand and then refer to their screenshot. hopefully that mostly dodges the issue.
2
3:59
or they could write the names of the cards they saw in TTS's chat

TheBigBoy 11/02/2026 4:03

4:04

4:05

fruit 11/02/2026 4:06
I like silly ideas. Some sort of cutoff could be part of a cool format
4:08
The format that we had the most fun with and agreed on being the fairest while still soft capping the amount of time spend on a game is capping each turn or decision during your opponents turn at 90 seconds that reset for each turn. You get 2 free strikes for taking longer. Your 3rd strike is an automatic loss
4:09
we even tested it with a new player on her second game ever. She managed to stay within the time limit and the game didnt take too long (shorter than 45 min) (edited)
4:11
there is no opportunity for stalling and the game is artificially held within 60 mins just by using the game design.

TMind 15/02/2026 5:40
When do I resolve a covered "when covered" effect with Metal 6 being removed by being covered ? (edited)
henri 15/02/2026 5:44
would be covered activates when a card would cover it, but then is performed while the card that would have covered it is in a committed state. So if hate 4 is covered by metal 6 and you play a card onto metal 6 first six is removed, put into the trash, then the hate four would be covered and the committed card is still committed while hate 4 activates, hate 4 itself not being a covered card yet.
5:45
Then after hate 4 resolves, the played card is put onto the stack, covering hate 4

Cosmic 15/02/2026 14:02
Spirit 1. If I have no cards in my hand, do I need to flip? Or can I choose to discard a card.
fruit 15/02/2026 14:05
You would need to flip
1
Thrixu
Question... psychic is played, rearrange their protocols. One the second picture you see my fire card moved to plague. Because they are moved... can i compile next turn plague ? Or not because they are fire cards ? Thanks in advance for the answer
StGrimblefig 15/02/2026 17:35
Once the cards are in play, there is no requirement that their protocol matches the protocol of the line. That restriction only applies to where you can play a card face-up from your hand. So yes, you would compile Plague on your turn.
1

Michael Yang 19/02/2026 18:21
Game state has to change for the effect to happen. For example, if I have a flip but the only target is <ice 4>, I can't flip and don't hit the window "After you flip a card:" It's the same thing with shuffling a deck with 0 or 1 card. The game state can't actually change so nothing happens.
4
1
Michael Yang
Game state has to change for the effect to happen. For example, if I have a flip but the only target is <ice 4>, I can't flip and don't hit the window "After you flip a card:" It's the same thing with shuffling a deck with 0 or 1 card. The game state can't actually change so nothing happens.

Michael Yang 22/02/2026 21:04
As it stands, I think the rules here are ambiguous. Specifically, there's room for interpretation between the rule that game state must change and the rule that says you can't refresh with 5+ cards in hand. We can infer some intention about the refresh rule but that isn't explicitly spelled out. My proposed solution is to make the refresh limitation rule a little more broad and more inline with the game-state-change rule: You can't refresh if you would draw no cards. This makes it clear that, in the Ice 6 example, I can't refresh, as well as the case where my deck and trash is empty, I can't refresh. This makes sense to me but I'm curious about more thoughts. This ruling would change my response to that linked BGG post.
1
BlueMoons 22/02/2026 21:05
hmm
21:06
i need to read the codex state rule again one second
21:07
so the game state thing is under the 'card text' section
Michael Yang
As it stands, I think the rules here are ambiguous. Specifically, there's room for interpretation between the rule that game state must change and the rule that says you can't refresh with 5+ cards in hand. We can infer some intention about the refresh rule but that isn't explicitly spelled out. My proposed solution is to make the refresh limitation rule a little more broad and more inline with the game-state-change rule: You can't refresh if you would draw no cards. This makes it clear that, in the Ice 6 example, I can't refresh, as well as the case where my deck and trash is empty, I can't refresh. This makes sense to me but I'm curious about more thoughts. This ruling would change my response to that linked BGG post.
makario 22/02/2026 21:07
That makes sense to me. I wouldn't have expected the bgg ruling
BlueMoons 22/02/2026 21:08
makario 22/02/2026 21:08
Seems the above change is indeed more consistent with fewer caveats (edited)
Michael Yang 22/02/2026 21:09
The problem there is that I try to be a rules lawyer when answering rules questions, not a designer. So my lawyer-self doesn't care about my designer-self's intention. My lawyer self saw that the limitation on refreshing is based on hand size and that the game state would change because of control so the play was legal. (edited)
BlueMoons 22/02/2026 21:10
the nuances around what counts as an unchanged game state may need some deeping out
Michael Yang 22/02/2026 21:10
That said, the intention is clear: you shouldn't be able to refresh with a full hand.

Microbike 28/02/2026 6:35
If I have fire 0 and apathy 4 in play, can I play smoke 1, flip apathy 4, shift it onto fire 0, and flip apathy 4 back up? Or can fire zero not target apathy 4 because it hasn't been committed yet
Microbike
If I have fire 0 and apathy 4 in play, can I play smoke 1, flip apathy 4, shift it onto fire 0, and flip apathy 4 back up? Or can fire zero not target apathy 4 because it hasn't been committed yet
Impalsi 28/02/2026 6:39
Fire 0 cannot target apathy 4 because it is committed when that trigger happens and is therefore not in play. Apathy 4 will land after Fire 0's trigger resolves

Impalsi
Do the cards played from Life 0 come in one at a time? Could you choose to cover Life 0 with the first card and intentionally NOT play cards in your other lines?
rfoconnor 02/03/2026 0:35
My reading is that all the cards come in at once, because it's just one sentence.
0:37

Impalsi 02/03/2026 1:21
turns out this one is covered explicitly in the codex
1
1:22
When Life 0's middle command triggers, the owner notes the lines that need to be acted in. Then, they choose which line to process, one at a time. For each line, they play the top card of their deck face-down, processing the consequences of that card play before addressing the next line. If Life 0 gets covered during this process, its middle command stops.
 (edited)
ThomasH 02/03/2026 10:01

NotWhale 02/03/2026 10:18

NotWhale

Impalsi 02/03/2026 10:20
Yep. Everything does. Or when it gets deleted or flipped

What is the exact order of operations when a card is deleted while <hate 3> is active? Is it: 1. A card is targeted for deletion (e.g. via “delete 1 card”) 2. The targeted card is committed. 3. All subsequent effects are resolved (e.g. due to the targeted card leaving play, and revealing middle text on another card). 4. The targeted card enters the trash. 5. Hate 3 triggers. I’m wondering if 5 should happen between 3 and 4, or simultaneously with 3.

BluhIce 06/03/2026 0:27

BluhIce 06/03/2026 0:28

1
Impalsi 06/03/2026 0:34
given the use of the word "discard" i would be shocked if it didn't count
0:35
but also, i see where in the MN02 rulebook discard is defined specifically as "from hand"
0:35
0:35
soooooo idk. lemme see if it's been mentioned in this channel before
0:37
not that i can see
0:38
maybe @Michael Yang could weigh in?
Michael Yang 06/03/2026 0:47
Yes, Corruption 2 would trigger because it's looking for the use of the discard keyword. The keywords define defaults for how cards move around. Draw means a card moves from your deck to your hand, Discard means your hand to your trash, and Delete means from a stack to its owner's trash. Some cards will modify parts of the keyword you're using. EG. "Play 1 card" moves a card from your hand to a stack by default. "Play the top card of your deck" modifies the first part but not the second, a card moves from the top of your deck to a stack. "Play the top card of your deck face-down on your opponent´s side." modifies both sides of the Play keyword.
3
1

ぱうぷる 15/03/2026 3:31
Move Gravity 2 on Life 3 with the end effect of Speed 3. In that case, the effect of Gravity 1 will be activated, and Speed 3 will be moved over Gravity 1. At this time, the speed 0 effect will be activated, but will this play get stuck while waiting?
3:34
will it be like this?
Impalsi 15/03/2026 3:40
@ぱうぷる it goes down like this: Speed 3 end effect triggers, targets Gravity 2 Gravity 2 is shifted, becomes committed to Line 3 (Life) Gravity 1 triggers, you draw 2 cards. It sounds like you want to shift Speed 3 with Gravity 1, so I'll proceed with that. Speed 3 is shifted, becomes committed to Line 1 (Gravity). Speed 0 triggers. It looks like you want to play Gravity 0 into Line 1 using this trigger, so I'll proceed with that. Gravity 0 must enter the committed queue on top of Speed 3 since there is already a card in queue. This is all Speed 0 can do right now, so it is finished. Next, Speed 3 lands in Line 1 (Gravity). Next, Gravity 0 lands in Line 1 (Gravity). Gravity 0 triggers and its trigger is resolved as normal. Next, Life 3 triggers and its trigger is resolved as normal. Next, Gravity 2 lands in Line 3 (Life).
2
Impalsi
@ぱうぷる it goes down like this: Speed 3 end effect triggers, targets Gravity 2 Gravity 2 is shifted, becomes committed to Line 3 (Life) Gravity 1 triggers, you draw 2 cards. It sounds like you want to shift Speed 3 with Gravity 1, so I'll proceed with that. Speed 3 is shifted, becomes committed to Line 1 (Gravity). Speed 0 triggers. It looks like you want to play Gravity 0 into Line 1 using this trigger, so I'll proceed with that. Gravity 0 must enter the committed queue on top of Speed 3 since there is already a card in queue. This is all Speed 0 can do right now, so it is finished. Next, Speed 3 lands in Line 1 (Gravity). Next, Gravity 0 lands in Line 1 (Gravity). Gravity 0 triggers and its trigger is resolved as normal. Next, Life 3 triggers and its trigger is resolved as normal. Next, Gravity 2 lands in Line 3 (Life).
ぱうぷる 15/03/2026 3:42
thank you!!!
1
ぱうぷる
thank you!!!
Impalsi 15/03/2026 3:43
you're welcome! hopefully that all makes sense. happy to clarify if you have any more questions.
ぱうぷる 15/03/2026 4:19
I'll ask you an additional question. Is the order in which the committed cards are placed on the line committed? (edited)
ぱうぷる
I'll ask you an additional question. Is the order in which the committed cards are placed on the line committed? (edited)
Impalsi 15/03/2026 4:46
I don't understand the question. I'll take a stab at answering and see if I get lucky:
  • Committed cards enter a queue, FIFO (first in first out). So in your screenshot above, once everything is done resolving, it'll be: Grav 0, all the facedown cards Grav 0 played, Speed 3, Grav 1. Grav 0 will be the uncovered card in the stack.
4:46
If that didn't answer the question, would you please rephrase?
Impalsi
@ぱうぷる it goes down like this: Speed 3 end effect triggers, targets Gravity 2 Gravity 2 is shifted, becomes committed to Line 3 (Life) Gravity 1 triggers, you draw 2 cards. It sounds like you want to shift Speed 3 with Gravity 1, so I'll proceed with that. Speed 3 is shifted, becomes committed to Line 1 (Gravity). Speed 0 triggers. It looks like you want to play Gravity 0 into Line 1 using this trigger, so I'll proceed with that. Gravity 0 must enter the committed queue on top of Speed 3 since there is already a card in queue. This is all Speed 0 can do right now, so it is finished. Next, Speed 3 lands in Line 1 (Gravity). Next, Gravity 0 lands in Line 1 (Gravity). Gravity 0 triggers and its trigger is resolved as normal. Next, Life 3 triggers and its trigger is resolved as normal. Next, Gravity 2 lands in Line 3 (Life).

Impalsi
I don't understand the question. I'll take a stab at answering and see if I get lucky:
  • Committed cards enter a queue, FIFO (first in first out). So in your screenshot above, once everything is done resolving, it'll be: Grav 0, all the facedown cards Grav 0 played, Speed 3, Grav 1. Grav 0 will be the uncovered card in the stack.

Eris
A doubt I would have if I was playing this. Since Gravity 1 has two separate actions, would Gravity 2 land in Line 3 after the player drawing 2 cards before the second effect begins? Kind if like if Spirit 3 is covering Spirit 0 and gets shifted, it lands after the "refresh" effect is resolved and then it can be shifted again by the second "draw a card effect"?
Impalsi 15/03/2026 14:03
No because in this case, the original shift was the first thing to happen and that part resolves as a LIFO (last in, first out) stack. So anything that's triggered after it resolves first
14:05
Contrast to spirit 3 where the most recent thing is spirit 3's trigger, so that resolves first. The most recent thing to trigger is the first thing to resolve.
Impalsi
No because in this case, the original shift was the first thing to happen and that part resolves as a LIFO (last in, first out) stack. So anything that's triggered after it resolves first
Eris 15/03/2026 14:08
I see, my confusion comes from thinking that when there's a dot the next effect won't start until everything pending is resolved
Impalsi
Contrast to spirit 3 where the most recent thing is spirit 3's trigger, so that resolves first. The most recent thing to trigger is the first thing to resolve.
Eris 15/03/2026 14:10

Eris
I see, my confusion comes from thinking that when there's a dot the next effect won't start until everything pending is resolved
Impalsi 15/03/2026 14:10

14:11

Eris

Impalsi 15/03/2026 14:11

14:12

Impalsi
Both the refresh and the draw on Spirit 0 will execute entirely before spirit 3 lands

Impalsi 15/03/2026 14:15

14:15
But yeah, spirit 3 being shifted off spirit 0 ... Both refresh and draw will execute before spirit 3 lands. So it will see neither (edited)
14:18

Sycopath 16/03/2026 0:40
Nitpicky question, the clarity cards that reveal your deck and draw a value one or five (clarity 2 & 3 respectively). The 1/5 drawn with these effects are drawn in a revealed state, correct? And for clarity 2 does the 1 need to be played face up? Or can it be played face down? (edited)
Sycopath
Nitpicky question, the clarity cards that reveal your deck and draw a value one or five (clarity 2 & 3 respectively). The 1/5 drawn with these effects are drawn in a revealed state, correct? And for clarity 2 does the 1 need to be played face up? Or can it be played face down? (edited)
Impalsi 16/03/2026 0:57
Great questions that I don't know the answer to. Very interesting. I would assume the answers are: Q: the clarity cards that reveal your deck and draw a value one or five. The 1/5 drawn with these effects are drawn in a revealed state, correct? in other words: If I revealed multiple 1s/5s, does my opponent get to know which I'm adding? A: I would place a hefty bet on "Yes, they are drawn revealed. Your opponent gets to know." But I cannot cite any authoritative source that backs this up, and would love to hear from or see such a source. Q: And for clarity 1, does the 1 it draws need to be played face up? A: This one actually seems more clear to me. From the rulesheet's section on what it means to "Play" a card: "That card can be played either face-up or face-down.". So I think we would be right to conclude that, unless otherwise specified, you can play it face-up or face-down. (edited)
Sycopath 16/03/2026 0:59
So my only misgiving about allowing a facedown play of the 1 is verification
Impalsi 16/03/2026 1:00
Ah, yep. It's like it would have to be revealed the whole time -- even as it's being played. Or else how can your opponent know for sure what you played face down? That is strange. (edited)
makario 16/03/2026 1:11
It is a good question. Especially since it's valid to play a different 1 than the one you drew.
1
Michael Yang 16/03/2026 1:21
For the visibility on the drawn cards, the intent is that both players get to see what cards are chosen. It doesn't necessarily address this problem directly but here's the current version of Clarity text: Reveal your deck and draw 1 card with a value of 5. Shuffle your deck. The reveal and draw are tied together with "and" to hopefully make this more clear.
1:27
For Clarity 2, the card has to be face-up. If you play a card face-down, you are playing a card with a value of 2. So the played card has to be face-up to have a value of 1. (edited)
3
Impalsi 16/03/2026 1:39

makario 16/03/2026 13:50

Impalsi
By similar reasoning: What is the value of a card in my deck? Face down, right? So 2? Does this mean Clarity 2 and 3 should always fail to find?
Michael Yang 16/03/2026 17:54
All the Clarity tutors behave the same way - you reveal the cards so people can see the values and agree on what they are when you take one. Otherwise, I don't know if talking about card values makes a lot of sense when they're hidden info and not in play. Here's a provoking follow-up question: If Light 0 flips Metal 6 and it ends up in the deck before the draw, what happens? I see 2 options: 0 cards (because we lose the reference to the card) or 2 cards because we assume that all of the cards in the deck have a default value of 2. I don't think it'd ever be 6 cards though. Curious about thoughts on this one? I lean toward 0 cards.
Impalsi

So if I conclude that cards in my deck have a value of 2, and I want to take things very literally and not try to guess at designer intent, then that makes Clarity 2 and 3 seem as though they would never find any card. coolduckwizard 16/03/2026 20:40

Michael Yang
Do you have a link to this rule in the MTG comprehensive rules?
BlueMoons 16/03/2026 22:00
113.7. The source of an ability is the object that generated it. The source of an activated ability on the stack is the object whose ability was activated. The source of a triggered ability (other than a delayed triggered ability) on the stack, or one that has triggered and is waiting to be put on the stack, is the object whose ability triggered. To determine the source of a delayed triggered ability, see rules 603.7d–f. 113.7a Once activated or triggered, an ability exists on the stack independently of its source. Destruction or removal of the source after that time won’t affect the ability. Note that some abilities cause a source to do something (for example, “This creature deals 1 damage to any target”) rather than the ability doing anything directly. In these cases, any activated or triggered ability that references information about the source for use while announcing an activated ability or putting a triggered ability on the stack checks that information when the ability is put onto the stack. Otherwise, it will check that information when it resolves. In both instances, if the source is no longer in the zone it’s expected to be in at that time, its last known information is used. The source can still perform the action even though it no longer exists.
so this is specifically regarding effects that are on the stack.
1
22:01
i.e. if you have a creature on board and the effect on the stack says "Destroy target creature. Draw cards equal to its power." you would draw cards based on the power it had on the field at the time it was destroyed, including any modifiers (edited)
